text: The surgeon begins reducing the direct right inguinal hernia, carefully peeling away the edge of the transversalis fascia.
The hernia sac is primarily composed of peritoneum and preperitoneal fat, with a possible inclusion of bladder tissue.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the edge of the hernia defect, reducing the hernia sac by moving laterally to locate the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon navigates through the hernia site, distinguishing between preperitoneal fat associated with the femoral space and fat descending with the hernia sac.
The surgeon continues the reduction of the direct right inguinal hernia, carefully manipulating the elongated hernia sac to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon cautiously presses down with scissors, attempting to form a right angle or gently grip the tissue with the scissor tip.
The surgeon identifies a small bleeder and pauses to ensure there is no further bleeding on the counter sac side, carefully monitoring the area for any retraction or additional bleeding.
The surgeon completes the reduction of a large hernia, with visible tissue manipulation and adjustment within the surgical field.
The surgeon crosses the midline to position the mesh beyond the medial edge of the direct defect, following extensive lateral dissection in the space of Bogros.
The surgeon combines the space of Retzius and the space of Bogros, navigating through a natural plane to avoid disturbing the iliac and femoral fat amidst abundant adipose tissue.
The surgeon aims to achieve a depth of at least two centimeters to ensure adequate posterior coverage of the mesh.
The surgeon begins dissection laterally over the testicular vessels, progressing medially into the pelvic region.
The surgeon observes bowel positioned closely against the peritoneum and avoids using cautery. The surgeon attempts to plicate the transversealis fascia by carefully grasping it.

The surgeon lifts a tissue layer and identifies an additional layer beneath it, deciding to incise the newly discovered layer. CO₂ is introduced into the space outside the countersack to facilitate reduction of the transverse salis. The surgeon considers removing the inner lining but opts not to due to its complexity.
The assistant switches to a needle driver, preparing to use a 3-0 V-Lock 180 suture. The surgeon attempts to take very small bites with the suture, although the application appears challenging.
The surgeon attempts to take small bites with the suture while pulling the transversalis fascia into the space, facing challenges due to the proximity of the genital branch.
The surgeon attempts to make a small purchase on the transversalis fascia, being cautious due to the proximity of the nerve on the opposite side of the fascia.
The surgeon aggressively incorporates the tip of the hernia sac with larger suture bites, causing the mass of the transversalis fascia to be pulled into the surgical space.

The surgeon sutures the transversalis fascia down to Cooper's ligament using a 3-0 V-lock suture, effectively reducing the bunching of the transversalis fascia. The V-lock suture secures the fascia in place, preventing slippage.
The surgeon takes another suture bite into Cooper's ligament to secure the counter sack and prevent it from retracting into the inguinal canal, creating a stable wall for mesh placement.
The surgeon locks the suture to prevent unraveling, completing the suturing process. Next, the surgeon prepares to place a 3D Max mid extra-large mesh into the surgical site.

The surgeon adjusts the position of the extra large Three D Max Mid mesh by shifting it laterally and rotating it clockwise to improve lateral coverage. A stitch is placed in Cooper's ligament to secure the mesh during the tap repair, ensuring the posterior edge overlap remains stable and prevents the mesh from lifting or sliding.

Using the robotic system, the surgeon places an additional stitch to secure the mesh over a large direct defect. The mesh is positioned well laterally, with the posterior lateral edge tucked into a pocket to prevent lifting, eliminating the need for an anterior lateral stitch.

The surgeon checks the posterior edge of the mesh to ensure it is properly positioned in the retropubic space and tucked under the visible hernia sac. The peritoneal defect is closed using a V-lock suture, with a focus on ensuring adequate coverage.

The surgeon uses the MaxMid mesh, aiming for optimal apposition of the peritoneum against the mesh by decreasing the pneumoperitoneum to 8 mmHg to desufflate the pocket. CO₂ is pressed out of the preperitoneal space before closing the defect, then the pneumoperitoneum is increased back to 15 mmHg to achieve a suction-like effect for mesh adherence.
The peritoneum is elevated and positioned well, with a visible large hole that is left as is. The tip of the hernia sac remains in place without additional suturing to the peritoneal flap.
